channel.title: NYT > Arts
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: John Lithgow as Roald Dahl in ‘Giant’: A Study in Monstrosity
item.description: In Mark Rosenblatt’s play, a powerful portrayal of the beloved children’s book author who almost gleefully exposes his bigotry.
item.dc:creator: Helen Shaw
item.media:credit: Sara Krulwich/The New York Times
item.media:description: John Lithgow in “Giant” at the Music Box Theater in Manhattan.

item.title: Comedy’s Most Manipulative Shot … and Its Greatest Weapon
item.description: Audience reactions are a staple of standup specials. But they’re a strange device when you take a closer look.
item.dc:creator: Jason Zinoman

item.title: Is It Possible to Solve the Ending of Puccini’s ‘Turandot’?
item.description: This Orientalist classic, left unfinished, has had to be completed since Puccini’s death. A new version hands that task to artists with Asian roots.
item.dc:creator: Kevin Ng
item.media:credit: Hiroyuki Ito for The New York Times
item.media:description: Puccini’s “Turandot” at the Metropolitan Opera, in Franco Zeffirelli’s production.

item.title: The Final, Flying Colors of Matisse’s ‘Second Life’
item.description: A breathtaking Paris show challenges the conventional idea that artists taper off at the end of their lives.
item.dc:creator: Emily LaBarge

item.title: Brooklyn Museum’s African Art Galleries to Unite Continent
item.description: With the planned creation of new galleries for its Arts of Africa collection, the Brooklyn Museum hopes visitors will see their cultures “represented with dignity.”
item.dc:creator: Aruna D’Souza

item.title: 11 Robyn Bangers for the Pop Star’s Long-Awaited Return
item.description: Prepare for her first album since 2018, “Sexistential,” with 49 minutes of up-tempo gems.
item.dc:creator: Caryn Ganz
item.media:credit: Ana Cuba for The New York Times
item.media:description: Robyn is back with “Sexistential,” her first new album since 2018.

item.title: Bill Cosby Loses Sex Assault Lawsuit and Faces a $59 Million Judgment
item.description: A California jury found that Mr. Cosby had abused Donna Motsinger in 1972 after inviting her to attend one of his comedy shows.
item.dc:creator: Graham Bowley and Rachel Parsons
item.media:credit: Mark Makela/Getty Images
item.media:description: Bill Cosby was accused in the civil suit of sexually assaulting Donna Motsinger in 1972 when he was a celebrated entertainer and she was a waitress in a restaurant he visited.

item.title: ‘Real Housewives’ Has Been a Meme Gold Mine for 20 Years
item.description: The Bravo TV empire, which turns 20 this month, has also been a gold mine for the internet.
item.dc:creator: Derrick Bryson Taylor
item.category: The Real Housewives of Orange County (TV Program)
item.media:credit: Photo Illustration by Elizabeth Renstrom for The New York Times

item.title: At ‘Baywatch’ Tryouts, Hoping to Be the Next Pam Anderson or Jason Momoa
item.description: Buff, waxed or otherwise, thousands answered an open casting call to become the next “Baywatch” discovery — or just “Drowning Swimmer No. 2.”
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.media:credit: Sinna Nasseri for The New York Times

item.title: A Review of the U.K.’s ‘S.N.L.’ Debut From Both Sides of the Atlantic
item.description: In a country with little knowledge of the American TV institution, the show landed in a new light. An American and a British critic found plenty to enjoy.
item.dc:creator: Jason Zinoman and Gabriel Tate

item.title: The Man Who Taught Boy Bands How to Move
item.description: A new tour featuring New Edition and Boyz II Men is also a showcase for the influential work of the longtime choreographer Brooke Payne.
item.dc:creator: Brian Seibert
item.media:credit: Graham Dickie for The New York Times

item.title: Review: On ‘Arirang,’ BTS Is Too Big to Fail. But Not Too Big to Succeed.
item.description: The K-pop superstars’ new album, “Arirang,” comes with huge commercial expectations. The group made room for artistic experimentation, too.
item.dc:creator: Jon Caramanica
item.media:credit: Pool photo by Kim Min-Hee
item.media:description: The seven members of BTS returned to the stage on Saturday in Seoul (though one skipped most of the choreography: An injured RM was mostly confined to a stool).

item.title: Why Felix Gonzalez-Torres’s Go-Go Dancer Piece Remains Subversive
item.description: A work about gay visibility avoids statements, yet remains powerful. A dancer appears just once a day, showing the political valence of absence.
item.dc:creator: Travis Diehl and Marcus Maddox
item.media:credit: Marcus Maddox for The New York Times

item.title: Two Choreographers, Two Visions of Philadelphia Freedom
item.description: Rennie Harris’s “Losing My Religion” and a Matthew Neenan program from BalletX were signs of health in two of the city’s most important dance institutions.
item.dc:creator: Brian Seibert

item.title: Write It Up! How Fast Do Our Journalists Type?
item.description: When it comes to breaking news, it’s a race not only to get the story, but to record it. We tracked down some of the speediest fingers in our newsroom.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Bahr

item.title: Late Night Questions the ‘Very Big Present’ Trump Got From Iran
item.description: Josh Johnson was puzzled by what kind of gift the president could have received “from the people you are currently at war with.”
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: The Daily Show with Jon Stewart (TV Program)
Late Night with Seth Meyers (TV Program)

item.title: Through Bamboo, the Artist Lap-See Lam Explores Her Family’s History
item.description: In a homecoming of sorts, Lap-See Lam has brought her multidisciplinary works to Hong Kong for her first solo show in Asia.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Russeth

item.title: Mike Vernon, Who Helped Spark the British Blues Boom, Dies at 81
item.description: He produced albums — by John Mayall & the Bluesbreakers, with Eric Clapton, and the early Fleetwood Mac — that defined 1960s blues rock. He also shepherded David Bowie’s debut disk.
item.dc:creator: Alex Williams
item.media:credit: Dick Barnatt/Redferns, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Mike Vernon in 1979. He produced albums for Britain’s powerhouse Decca label as well as for his own label, Blue Horizon.

item.title: Robert White, Tenor Renowned for Irish Ballads, Dies at 89
item.description: A performer from childhood, he became a versatile singer in the mold of the Irish superstar John McCormack, adept at both classical repertoire and traditional songs.
item.dc:creator: James Barron

item.title: David A. Ross Sought Epstein’s Help to Build Art Museums. Now He’s Facing the Fallout.
item.description: He mastered the world of the “Epstein Class” to build great museums. Now he’s confronting the cost.
item.dc:creator: Eli Saslow and Erin Schaff
item.category: San Francisco Museum of Modern Art

item.title: Tango Therapy: How the Dance of Passion Is Helping Parkinson’s Patients
item.description: Once a week, patients in an Argentine hospital with Parkinson’s disease use the movements of tango to help address issues of balance, stiffness and coordination.
item.dc:creator: Magalí Druscovich and Pam Belluck

item.title: Wicked Stepmother No Longer, a Female Pharoah Gets a Reputational Makeover
item.description: A reassessment of damaged 3,500-year-old statuary adds to evidence that Queen Hatshepsut wasn’t the villain that scholars long took her to be.
item.dc:creator: Franz Lidz

item.title: Late Night Doesn’t Feel So Hot About ICE at the Airport
item.description: Jimmy Kimmel said President Trump had “found a way to make the airport even worse.”
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: Late Night with Seth Meyers (TV Program)
Real Time With Bill Maher (TV Program)

item.title: How to Reduce the Environmental Impact of Collecting
item.description: From flying commercial to choosing reusable packing crates, art enthusiasts have many options to consider
item.dc:creator: Nina Siegal
item.media:credit: Ben Pearce

item.title: Gold, A New Art Space in Hong Kong, Opens
item.description: Recalling the era of Picasso and Hemingway, the location in Hong Kong, called Gold, will host art, design and fashion exhibitions, and more.
item.dc:creator: Dean Napolitano

item.title: A Painter Reveals Hong Kong’s Natural Wonders — and Where to See Them
item.description: Growing up, Stephen Wong Chun Hei did not get outside much. Now, he hikes the city’s trails and renders them in wild color. Here are six of his favorites.
item.dc:creator: Anjali Tsui

item.title: Outside Art Basel Hong Kong, Cyborgs, Abstractions and More
item.description: The city’s museums and galleries entice with work by boldfaced names like Lee Bul and Mary Weatherford, plus shows on textiles and collective memory.
item.dc:creator: Mercedes Hutton
item.media:credit: Lee Bul via M+, Hong Kong
item.media:description: Works by Lee Bul on display at M+. The exhibition includes pieces that explore the aspirations (and often follies) of utopian endeavors, along with works from her seminal “Cyborg” series.

item.title: Exploring Hong Kong’s Textile Trail
item.description: At the peak, Hong Kong was one of Asia’s largest textile exporters. Now, visitors can see tangible reminders of that history in several neighborhoods.
item.dc:creator: Christy Choi

item.title: Valerie Perrine, Screen Siren Who Won Critical Acclaim, Dies at 82
item.description: Known early on for skin-baring temptress roles, she later earned rave reviews, a Cannes award and an Oscar nomination for her performance in the Lenny Bruce biopic “Lenny.”
item.dc:creator: Alex Williams
item.media:credit: Chic Donchin/American Broadcasting Companies, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Valerie Perrine in “Malibu,” a 1983 television movie on ABC. “Unlike most sex symbols of the past,” one critic wrote, she “has almost always been singled out by the critics for her acting rather than her anatomy.”

item.title: Augie Meyers, Pioneer of Tex-Mex Music, Dies at 85
item.description: His carnival-like swirls on the Vox organ helped define the sound of the border with groups like the Sir Douglas Quintet and the Texas Tornados.
item.dc:creator: Alex Williams
item.media:credit: Gie Knaeps/Getty Images
item.media:description: Augie Meyers in 1983. His bright, syncopated riffs on the Vox organ functioned as the sonic engine of the Sir Douglas Quintet and set the model for countless groups to follow.

item.title: What’s It Like to Be Back in Print After 20 Years? A Bit Odd.
item.description: Nancy Lemann published her first novel at 28. Then came “the doom.” Now she’s back in the spotlight, and not exactly comfortable with it.
item.dc:creator: Marie Solis
item.media:credit: Annie Flanagan for The New York Times
item.media:description: The author outside the apartment in New Orleans where she wrote “Lives of the Saints.”

item.title: New Yorkers, Have You Found Your Microscene?
item.description: A sampling of the city’s creative cohorts and the places where they gather, from the Tompkins Square Park monkey bars to a hair salon that doubles as an art gallery.
item.dc:creator: Kate Guadagnino, Nick Haramis and Jennifer Livingston

item.title: ‘Something Very Bad Is Going to Happen,’ Plus 6 Things to Watch on TV This Week
item.description: A new horror thriller premieres and Miley Cyrus celebrates the 20th anniversary of “Hannah Montana.”
item.dc:creator: Shivani Gonzalez
item.media:credit: Courtesy of Netflix
item.media:description: Adam DiMarco and Camila Morrone in “Something Very Bad Is Going to Happen.”

item.title: ‘The Comeback’ Season 3 Premiere Recap: The Old Razzle-Dazzle
item.description: The HBO comedy returned for a new season on Sunday, more than 11 years after the previous one.
item.dc:creator: Noel Murray
item.media:credit: Erin Simkin/HBO
item.media:description: Lisa Kudrow is back for a new season of “The Comeback” on HBO.

item.title: For ‘Buffy’ Fans, Nicholas Brendon’s Xander Was a Complicated Everyman
item.description: The actor, who died on Friday, was a fan favorite on “Buffy the Vampire Slayer,” even as his character mixed quiet heroism with hostility toward the show’s women.
item.dc:creator: Derrick Bryson Taylor
item.media:credit: 20th Century Fox, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Nicholas Brendon, bottom left, as Xander Harris in “Buffy the Vampire Slayer.” His castmates included Alyson Hannigan, top left, Charisma Carpenter, right, and Sarah Michelle Gellar, center.

item.title: Christopher Columbus Statue Is Installed on White House Grounds
item.description: The statue of the explorer, a replica of one that protesters toppled in 2020, was placed outside the Eisenhower Executive Office Building.
item.dc:creator: Julia Jacobs
item.media:credit: Jason Andrew for The New York Times
item.media:description: A statue of Christopher Columbus was installed overnight in front of the Eisenhower Executive Office Building in Washington on Sunday.

item.title: Taylor Frankie Paul and the Downfall of the ‘Bachelor’ Empire
item.description: As competing dating shows lean into modern norms, the ABC franchise finds itself mired in scandals and stymied by its traditional approach to love.
item.dc:creator: Reggie Ugwu and Shivani Gonzalez

item.title: Choose Your Netflix Mystery: Agatha Christie or Jo Nesbo
item.description: New international series offer British sleuthing, Nordic noir, anime heartbreak and the ethereal landscapes of China’s west.
item.dc:creator: Mike Hale

item.title: Who Is Antigone? The 2500-Year-Old Rebel With a Cause.
item.description: “Antigone” gave us the original “bad girl,” but its themes go beyond that. How do adaptations keep making Sophocles’ ideas about democracy and theater new?
item.dc:creator: Helen Shaw

item.title: Why Are We Obsessed With Antigone?
item.description: “Antigone,” an ancient Greek play, is being adapted in several theaters across New York City. Our critic Helen Shaw explains why Sophocles’ anti-heroine is such a relevant figure today.
item.dc:creator: Helen Shaw, Léo Hamelin and Laura Salaberry

item.title: K-Pop’s BTS Returns to the Stage
item.description: The group’s first concert in nearly four years — held in the historic center of Seoul — was heavy on songs from its new album. They also played a few old hits.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.media:credit: Jun Michael Park for The New York Times

item.title: The director of the Netflix livestream is a veteran of the Oscars and the Super Bowl.
item.description: Hamish Hamilton, a multiple-time Emmy and BAFTA winner, is directing the Netflix broadcast of BTS’s comeback concert on Saturday.
item.dc:creator: Jin Yu Young
item.media:credit: Phil McCarten/Invision, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Hamish Hamilton, an accomplished television director who specializes in live broadcasts, accepting an Emmy Award in 2024.

item.title: Behind the scenes of the BTS livestream.
item.dc:creator: Jin Yu Young
item.media:credit: Jun Michael Park for The New York Times
item.media:description: Gwanghwamun Square in Seoul, where the concert is taking place on Saturday.

item.title: Why BTS Is Promoting Its New Album, ‘Arirang,’ in Korea Before Hitting the Road
item.description: The last time BTS released an album, it kicked off with appearances in New York before Seoul. This time it’s the other way around.
item.dc:creator: Jeff Benjamin
item.media:credit: Jun Michael Park for The New York Times
item.media:description: BTS merchandise at a convenient store in Seoul on Saturday.

item.title: What BTS Wore for Its Comeback Concert and Why
item.description: The band’s looks, by the Korean brand Songzio, represented a bigger story about Korean culture and meaning.
item.dc:creator: Vanessa Friedman

item.title: Here’s how America met BTS.
item.description: The South Korean band broke into American pop culture by hitting the talk shows, performing on “Saturday Night Live” and appearing at the White House.
item.dc:creator: Francesca Regalado
item.media:credit: Doug Mills/The New York Times
item.media:description: Members of BTS joined the White House press secretary, Karine Jean-Pierre, at the White House, in 2022.

item.title: There was extra security as BTS fans arrived at the concert site.
item.dc:creator: Max Kim

item.title: One reason BTS went on hiatus? To do military service.
item.dc:creator: Choe Sang-Hun
item.media:credit: Shin Yong-Ju/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: BTS members RM, left, and V in Chuncheon, South Korea, last year, after their release from 18 months of military service.

item.title: ​Gwanghwamun Square, the venue of the show, has long attracted crowds.
item.dc:creator: Choe Sang-Hun
item.media:credit: Chang W. Lee/The New York Times
item.media:description: A protest against then President Yoon Suk Yeol of South Korea in Gwanghwamun Square in 2024.

item.title: Here’s the latest.
item.dc:creator: Jin Yu Young, Yan Zhuang, Jeff Benjamin and Max Kim

item.title: St. Vincent, Death Cab for Cutie: 8 Songs We’re Talking About This Week
item.description: St. Vincent teams with a 60-piece orchestra, and Squarepusher replaces one by playing all the parts himself.
item.dc:creator: Jon Pareles
item.media:credit: OK McCausland for The New York Times
item.media:description: St. Vincent’s “Live in London!” captures a performance conducted by Jules Buckley.

item.title: Art Basel Hong Kong: Injecting Energy Into the Art Fair Experience
item.description: A four-person team has shaped the Encounters section of Art Basel Hong Kong, organizing it around the elements of water, fire, earth and ether.
item.dc:creator: Bonnie Tsui

item.title: At Hong Kong Art Basel, an Exhibition of Works by El Anatsui
item.description: The artist El Anatsui has returned to his roots in Ghana and is showing in Hong Kong during Art Basel.
item.dc:creator: Farah Nayeri

item.title: A Navy Veteran Finds His Niche in the Art World
item.description: It took courage for Walter Price to move to New York and pursue a career as a painter, but he says his dream has come true.
item.dc:creator: Ted Loos
item.media:credit: Oliver Farshi for The New York Times
item.media:description: Contemporary painter Walter Price at his studio in Bushwick, Brooklyn.

item.title: Scott Evans Recommends These Books to the Women in His Life
item.description: The “Access Hollywood” and “House Guest” host shares his love of “Jesus Christ Superstar,” Keke Palmer and celebrity memoirs that go deep.
item.dc:creator: Leigh-Ann Jackson
item.media:credit: Willy Sanjuan/Invision, via Associated Press

item.title: Nicholas Brendon, Beloved Sidekick on ‘Buffy the Vampire Slayer,’ Dies at 54
item.description: He played the part of Xander Harris, one of Buffy’s closest friends, on the hit television show about a teenage girl who protects the world from monsters.
item.dc:creator: Francesca Regalado and Hannah Ziegler
item.media:credit: Barry Brecheisen/WireImage
item.media:description: Nicholas Brendon, who played the lovable sidekick on the hit show “Buffy the Vampire Slayer,” has died.

item.title: ‘The Bachelorette’ Took a Risk on Taylor Frankie Paul. It Backfired.
item.description: Ms. Paul’s built-in fan base and viral interpersonal drama presented an opportunity for the long-running reality TV show, until video of a past assault derailed the season.
item.dc:creator: Julia Jacobs and John Koblin
item.category: Mormons (Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-Day Saints)
The Secret Lives of Mormon Wives (TV Program)

item.title: Don DeLillo’s Hockey Novel, ‘Amazons,’ Will Return to Stores
item.description: You’re welcome.
item.dc:creator: Alexandra Alter

item.title: Chuck Norris: A Life in Pictures
item.description: The actor, known for his skills as a martial artist, often played the warrior onscreen.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.media:credit: Robert Voets/CBS, via Getty Images

item.title: Chuck Norris Punched This Article Into the Sun: How an Action Hero Became a Meme
item.description: Norris, best known as the butt-kicking star of action films, became an unwitting if good-natured pioneer of the internet meme.
item.dc:creator: Sopan Deb
item.media:credit: Sunset Boulevard/Getty Images
item.media:description: Chuck Norris in “Missing in Action” (1984). He once beat the sun in a staring contest, according to an internet meme from the mid-2000s.
